title: Capstone: End-to-End — Train a Tiny LLM, Serve It with vLLM and SGLang
description: The flagship capstone. From empty terminal to a curl-able LLM endpoint: TinyStories data prep, a 12M-param Llama-style transformer trained from scratch in ~20 minutes on a 4090 (or a free Colab T4), a custom Triton (CUDA) or torch.compile (MPS) kernel for the hot path, HuggingFace-format export, then production-grade serving with vLLM (PagedAttention) and SGLang (RadixAttention) in Docker, with full-stack debugging at every layer.

sections.title: Welcome — What We're Going to Build, and Why
sections.content:
This is the module the entire site has been pointing at. Every other module taught you one layer in isolation — tensors, autograd, attention, RMSNorm, RoPE, CUDA kernels, MPS, KV caches, PagedAttention, RadixAttention. This module makes you build the whole stack yourself, from an empty terminal, and run it.

### What you'll have at the end

A real, working pipeline that looks like this:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  DATA["TinyStories\n2.1M short stories\n~500M tokens"] --> TOK["Llama tokenizer\n(32k BPE)"]
  TOK --> BIN["train.bin / val.bin\n(uint16 token arrays)"]
  BIN --> TRAIN["PyTorch train.py\n12M-param Llama\n8 layers, hidden 256"]
  TRAIN --> CKPT["checkpoint.pt\nstate_dict + config"]
  CKPT --> HF["to_hf.py\nLlamaForCausalLM\nsave_pretrained()"]
  HF --> SAFE["model.safetensors\nconfig.json\ntokenizer.*"]
  SAFE --> VLLM["vLLM in Docker\nPagedAttention\n:8000/v1"]
  SAFE --> SGL["SGLang in Docker\nRadixAttention\n:30000/v1"]
  VLLM --> CURL["curl /v1/completions\nopenai-python client"]
  SGL --> CURL
```

The model is small (about 12M parameters — call it 0.0002 GPT-3s) but the **stack is real**. Every component is what you'd use to serve a 70B model, just sized down so you can iterate on a laptop GPU.

### Why train your own model when good ones already exist?

Because **you cannot understand the inference stack until you've trained the thing it serves**. Until you've felt the cosine warmup, debugged a NaN, written a custom kernel for the layer that's slow, and watched your loss go from 11.0 to 2.3, the inference layer is just configuration files. After you've trained your own, the words `dtype`, `kv_heads`, `rope_theta`, and `max_position_embeddings` mean *physical things* and the inference server's documentation reads like English.

The second reason: every problem you'll hit serving a real LLM in production has an analog in this miniature stack. Tokenizer drift. State-dict-key mismatches. KV cache OOMs. Triton kernels that compile but produce wrong numbers under autotune. Docker GPU passthrough breaking on a kernel upgrade. You want those bruises on a 12M-param model where the train-fix-retry loop is 20 minutes, not on a 70B-finetune that costs $4,000 per run.

### Hardware and time budget

| Setup | Time end-to-end | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| RTX 4090 (24 GB) | ~20 min training, ~5 min serving | The fast path. Use this if you have it. |
| RTX 3060 (12 GB) | ~40 min training | Reduce batch size by half. |
| M2 Max / M3 / M4 Mac | ~30 min training | MPS works; vLLM doesn't — serve via Colab. |
| Free Colab T4 | ~60 min training | The path this exercise is wired for. Token throughput ~half of 4090. |
| CPU only | A few hours | Possible. Use `--device cpu` and a shorter run. |

### What you need installed

On a local machine: `python>=3.10`, `pip`, `torch>=2.4`, `tiktoken` or `transformers`, `triton` (CUDA only), `safetensors`, `docker` (with `nvidia-container-toolkit` for GPU), `curl`. On Colab everything but Docker is pre-installed. We'll handle Docker by running the serving step locally after downloading the checkpoint from Colab.

### How to read this module

Each section is a fully-runnable step. You should be able to copy each code block into a file, run it, and move on. No box says "left as an exercise." The optional rabbit holes are flagged as **deep-dive** boxes and can be skipped on a first pass.

Let's go.

sections.title: Get and Prep the Dataset (TinyStories → packed token bins)
sections.content:
We need a corpus small enough to tokenize in minutes but real enough that a transformer trained on it produces coherent English. **TinyStories** is exactly that — a synthetic dataset of 2.1M short children's stories generated by GPT-3.5/4, designed by Eldan & Li (2023) specifically so a tiny transformer can learn coherent narrative on it.

Dataset URL: https://huggingface.co/datasets/roneneldan/TinyStories

### What the data looks like

Each example is a few hundred tokens of narrative — typical length ~200-400 tokens. The total corpus is roughly **500M tokens**. We'll use a small slice (~10M tokens) so the prep step runs in a couple of minutes.

```
Once upon a time, there was a tiny dog named Spot...
```

The vocabulary is intentionally simple — small words, limited grammar — which is what lets a 12M-param model produce readable output.

### Tokenizer: Llama 32k BPE, via HuggingFace

We will tokenize with the **Llama 2 tokenizer** (vocab size 32000, SentencePiece BPE). Two reasons:

1. It's the de facto standard for small Llama-style models.
2. When we later serve via vLLM / SGLang as a `LlamaForCausalLM`, the serving engine expects Llama's tokenizer files. If we tokenize with tiktoken (GPT-2 BPE) here and serve as Llama later, every prompt sent through the server will be a different token sequence than the model trained on — silent corruption.

**Lesson #1 of production LLMs**: the tokenizer is part of the model. Pin it from the start.

```python
# prep_data.py
import os
import numpy as np
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

OUT_DIR = './data'
os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

# Use the Llama-2 tokenizer (32k BPE). If gated, swap to a public mirror like
# 'NousResearch/Llama-2-7b-hf' or 'unsloth/llama-2-7b' — the tokenizer is the same.
TOKENIZER_NAME = 'unsloth/llama-2-7b'   # public, same tokenizer.json as Meta's
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(TOKENIZER_NAME)
assert tokenizer.vocab_size == 32000, f'expected 32000, got {tokenizer.vocab_size}'

# Stream the dataset so we don't blow RAM. We'll cap at ~10M training tokens.
TARGET_TRAIN_TOKENS = 10_000_000
TARGET_VAL_TOKENS   =    500_000

ds = load_dataset('roneneldan/TinyStories', split='train', streaming=True)

def tokenize_iter(stream, max_tokens):
    """Yield token ids until we've produced `max_tokens` total."""
    produced = 0
    eos = tokenizer.eos_token_id
    for ex in stream:
        ids = tokenizer.encode(ex['text'], add_special_tokens=False)
        ids.append(eos)            # separate stories with EOS
        for t in ids:
            yield t
            produced += 1
            if produced >= max_tokens:
                return

def write_bin(stream, path, max_tokens):
    # uint16 is enough — our vocab is 32k, fits in 16 bits.
    arr = np.fromiter(tokenize_iter(stream, max_tokens), dtype=np.uint16, count=max_tokens)
    arr.tofile(path)
    print(f'wrote {path}: {len(arr):,} tokens, {os.path.getsize(path)/1e6:.1f} MB')

write_bin(ds, f'{OUT_DIR}/train.bin', TARGET_TRAIN_TOKENS)
# Take the next slice for val
val_ds = load_dataset('roneneldan/TinyStories', split='validation', streaming=True)
write_bin(val_ds, f'{OUT_DIR}/val.bin', TARGET_VAL_TOKENS)
```

This is the **nanoGPT pattern**: tokenize once, save as a flat `uint16` array on disk. At training time, we'll `np.memmap` the file and slice random windows out of it. No DataLoader, no `__getitem__`, no Python overhead in the hot path.

### Memory math (sanity check before you run it)

10M training tokens × 2 bytes (uint16) = **20 MB** on disk. The validation set is 1 MB. The HuggingFace dataset cache will be larger (~1.5 GB of raw text) but you can delete that after `prep_data.py` finishes — the only thing you actually need going forward is the `.bin` files.

If 10M tokens sounds small: it's enough to get the model out of memorization range (12M params × 6 tokens per param Chinchilla-rule = 72M tokens for compute-optimal; we're under that, which means the model will be undertrained but still produce coherent stories). Bigger runs scale this linearly.

### Why a flat uint16 array

The Tiny LLM training loop will look like:

```python
data = np.memmap('data/train.bin', dtype=np.uint16, mode='r')
# 11M tokens addressable as a single array, mapped lazily from disk by the OS.
ix = torch.randint(len(data) - block_size, (batch_size,))
x = torch.stack([torch.from_numpy(data[i:i+block_size].astype(np.int64)) for i in ix])
y = torch.stack([torch.from_numpy(data[i+1:i+1+block_size].astype(np.int64)) for i in ix])
```

Three advantages over a HuggingFace `Dataset`:

1. **Constant-time random access.** Slicing into a `memmap` is O(1). You never load the file fully into RAM.
2. **No per-example tokenization overhead.** Already done, frozen, on disk.
3. **Trivial multi-GPU sharding.** Each rank seeds differently and slices into the same `memmap`.

If you wanted to go bigger (Pile, FineWeb), the only thing that changes is the shape of the data prep step — you'd shard across multiple `.bin` files. The training loop reads them identically.

#### Deep-dive: tiktoken vs SentencePiece

If you prefer `tiktoken` (faster pure-Python encoder, GPT-2/cl100k BPE) you absolutely can — the resulting model would still train fine on TinyStories. But then you would have to serve it via a custom HuggingFace tokenizer wrapper, because Llama's tokenizer files are SentencePiece-format and vLLM/SGLang load them via `LlamaTokenizer`. Either commit to tiktoken end-to-end (and write your own minimal HF tokenizer config), or use Llama's SentencePiece throughout. We pick the latter to keep the serving step boilerplate-free.

sections.title: The Model — A Llama-Style Transformer in ~150 Lines
sections.content:
This is the model. ~150 lines of PyTorch. It is a real Llama-2-style transformer, just small. The forward pass is exactly the one that runs in Meta's production checkpoint — same RMSNorm, same SwiGLU, same RoPE, same untied-attention-then-tied-embedding scheme. The point of writing it from scratch is that there is no magic later when we map state-dict keys to `LlamaForCausalLM`.

### Config

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class TinyLlamaConfig:
    vocab_size: int = 32000          # Llama tokenizer
    n_layers:   int = 8              # depth
    n_heads:    int = 8              # query heads
    n_kv_heads: int = 8              # KV heads (=n_heads here; no GQA at this scale)
    dim:        int = 256            # hidden / model dim
    ffn_dim:    int = 1024           # SwiGLU expansion (~4x usually; here 4x with rounding)
    block_size: int = 512            # max sequence length
    rope_theta: float = 10000.0      # RoPE base
    norm_eps:   float = 1e-5         # RMSNorm epsilon
```

~12M params. Quick arithmetic to convince yourself:

- token + position parameters: embedding `32000 × 256 = 8.2M` (the bulk of the model, oddly enough at this scale).
- per layer: attention `4 × 256 × 256 = 262k`; ffn `3 × 256 × 1024 = 786k`; norms negligible. Per layer ~1.05M.
- 8 layers: 8.4M.
- Total: **~16.6M** before weight tying. With embedding tied to `lm_head`, we save 8.2M, giving **~8.4M**. (If you don't tie, ~16.6M.) The exact count depends on bias toggles. We'll print the count at train time.

### RMSNorm — why not LayerNorm

RMSNorm drops the mean-centering step from LayerNorm. The math:

```python
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(dim))
        self.eps = eps
    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (..., dim)
        # rms = sqrt(mean(x^2) + eps)
        norm = torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)
        return self.weight * (x * norm)
```

LayerNorm computes `(x - mean(x)) / sqrt(var(x) + eps) * gamma + beta`. RMSNorm computes `x / sqrt(mean(x^2) + eps) * gamma`. Two ops dropped (mean subtraction, beta), one fewer reduction. About **10-30% faster** with no measurable quality loss in Zhang & Sennrich 2019 and the Llama papers. Pure simplification win.

### SwiGLU FFN — why not GELU

Classic transformer FFN: `down(act(up(x)))` where `act` is GELU or ReLU. Llama uses **SwiGLU**: `down(silu(gate(x)) * up(x))`, three projections instead of two:

```python
class SwiGLU(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, ffn_dim):
        super().__init__()
        # bias=False is a Llama convention (we keep it everywhere)
        self.w_gate = nn.Linear(dim, ffn_dim, bias=False)
        self.w_up   = nn.Linear(dim, ffn_dim, bias=False)
        self.w_down = nn.Linear(ffn_dim, dim, bias=False)
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.w_down(F.silu(self.w_gate(x)) * self.w_up(x))
```

Shazeer (2020) found gated variants consistently beat plain GELU on language modeling at equal parameter budgets. Llama picked it; everyone copied. **silu(x) = x * sigmoid(x)** is smooth and gradient-friendly.

### RoPE — rotary positional embedding

No learned positional embedding table. Instead, before the attention dot product, we rotate Q and K by an angle proportional to their position. The relative-position dot product `<rotate(q, i), rotate(k, j)>` depends only on `i-j`, which is what we want.

```python
def precompute_rope_freqs(dim_per_head, seq_len, theta=10000.0, device='cpu'):
    """Returns cos/sin tables of shape (seq_len, dim_per_head/2)."""
    # Inverse frequencies: 1/theta^(2i/d) for i = 0..d/2-1
    inv_freq = 1.0 / (theta ** (torch.arange(0, dim_per_head, 2, device=device).float() / dim_per_head))
    t = torch.arange(seq_len, device=device).float()
    freqs = torch.outer(t, inv_freq)            # (seq_len, dim/2)
    return freqs.cos(), freqs.sin()

def apply_rope(x, cos, sin):
    # x: (B, n_heads, T, d_head)
    # cos, sin: (T, d_head/2)
    x1, x2 = x[..., 0::2], x[..., 1::2]
    # Stack rotated halves back together interleaved.
    rot_x1 = x1 * cos - x2 * sin
    rot_x2 = x2 * cos + x1 * sin
    out = torch.stack((rot_x1, rot_x2), dim=-1).flatten(-2)
    return out
```

RoPE's win over learned positional embeddings: **extrapolation**. You train at `block_size=512` and can run inference at 2048 or beyond just by computing more cos/sin entries. No new parameters, no retraining. Llama-3 uses RoPE with `theta=500000` to extend context to 128k; we'll stick with `theta=10000` since we don't need long context.

### Attention block — no bias, no dropout

```python
class Attention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: TinyLlamaConfig):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_heads = cfg.n_heads
        self.n_kv_heads = cfg.n_kv_heads
        self.d_head = cfg.dim // cfg.n_heads
        # Note: separate Q/K/V projections (Llama style). bias=False everywhere.
        self.wq = nn.Linear(cfg.dim, cfg.n_heads    * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(cfg.dim, cfg.n_kv_heads * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(cfg.dim, cfg.n_kv_heads * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(cfg.n_heads * self.d_head, cfg.dim, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, cos, sin):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q = self.wq(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads,    self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, H, T, d)
        k = self.wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_kv_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_kv_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        # Apply RoPE on Q and K (NOT V).
        q = apply_rope(q, cos[:T], sin[:T])
        k = apply_rope(k, cos[:T], sin[:T])
        # If we had n_kv_heads < n_heads, we'd repeat_interleave here. We don't, so skip.
        # Flash-attention via PyTorch's SDPA. is_causal=True applies the upper-triangular mask
        # without materializing it (huge memory savings vs explicit mask).
        out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)            # (B, H, T, d)
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C)
        return self.wo(out)
```

Notes:

- **No bias on any linear.** Llama dropped biases everywhere in `LlamaForCausalLM`. Removes about 0.5% of params with no quality cost.
- **No attention dropout.** Llama 2/3 drop it. The model learns better without.
- **`scaled_dot_product_attention`** (PyTorch ≥ 2.0) routes to FlashAttention v2 on CUDA, the math kernel on MPS, and the naive kernel on CPU. We get FlashAttention for free without touching CUDA.

### The block and the full model

```python
class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: TinyLlamaConfig):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn_norm = RMSNorm(cfg.dim, cfg.norm_eps)
        self.attn = Attention(cfg)
        self.ffn_norm = RMSNorm(cfg.dim, cfg.norm_eps)
        self.ffn = SwiGLU(cfg.dim, cfg.ffn_dim)

    def forward(self, x, cos, sin):
        # Pre-norm: norm -> sublayer -> add to residual.  This is the Llama / GPT-NeoX layout.
        x = x + self.attn(self.attn_norm(x), cos, sin)
        x = x + self.ffn(self.ffn_norm(x))
        return x

class TinyLlama(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: TinyLlamaConfig):
        super().__init__()
        self.cfg = cfg
        self.tok_emb = nn.Embedding(cfg.vocab_size, cfg.dim)
        self.blocks = nn.ModuleList([Block(cfg) for _ in range(cfg.n_layers)])
        self.final_norm = RMSNorm(cfg.dim, cfg.norm_eps)
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(cfg.dim, cfg.vocab_size, bias=False)
        # WEIGHT TYING — share embedding and lm_head matrices.
        self.lm_head.weight = self.tok_emb.weight
        # Precompute RoPE tables for the max sequence length.
        cos, sin = precompute_rope_freqs(cfg.dim // cfg.n_heads, cfg.block_size, cfg.rope_theta)
        self.register_buffer('rope_cos', cos, persistent=False)
        self.register_buffer('rope_sin', sin, persistent=False)
        # Reasonable init.
        self.apply(self._init_weights)

    @staticmethod
    def _init_weights(m):
        if isinstance(m, nn.Linear):
            nn.init.normal_(m.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.02)
        elif isinstance(m, nn.Embedding):
            nn.init.normal_(m.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.02)

    def forward(self, idx, targets=None):
        # idx: (B, T) int64
        B, T = idx.shape
        assert T <= self.cfg.block_size, f'sequence {T} > block_size {self.cfg.block_size}'
        x = self.tok_emb(idx)                          # (B, T, dim)
        cos = self.rope_cos[:T]
        sin = self.rope_sin[:T]
        for blk in self.blocks:
            x = blk(x, cos, sin)
        x = self.final_norm(x)
        logits = self.lm_head(x)                       # (B, T, vocab)
        if targets is None:
            return logits, None
        loss = F.cross_entropy(
            logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1)),
            targets.view(-1),
            ignore_index=-1,
        )
        return logits, loss
```

### Why weight tying

The input embedding `tok_emb.weight` is shape `(vocab, dim)`. The output projection `lm_head.weight` is also shape `(vocab, dim)`. Press & Wolf (2017) noticed: at the input, you're looking up a row per token; at the output, you're computing a dot product per row. Same matrix, used twice. Tying them halves the parameter count of these two layers (which dominate small models) with essentially zero quality cost.

Llama-2-7B and larger do not tie (they have enough parameters that it's no longer the bottleneck). Llama-3.2 1B and 3B **do** tie. Tiny models almost always tie. We tie.

### KV cache awareness

Nothing in this code uses a KV cache yet — `forward` always recomputes K and V from scratch. That's correct for training. For inference we'd add an optional `kv_cache` argument and an `offset` for RoPE indexing. We won't bother — the inference serving step (vLLM / SGLang) reimplements the architecture in its own KV-cache-aware kernel. Our PyTorch model just needs to produce the right *weights*.

The one thing we have to get right is the **state-dict naming convention** so vLLM can load the weights. We'll fix that in section 6.

sections.title: Training Loop — Device Picker, AdamW, Cosine Schedule, torch.compile, MFU
sections.content:
Now the training loop. The goal is a single `train.py` that runs on **CUDA, MPS, or CPU** depending on what's available, prints useful metrics every step, and writes a checkpoint we can convert to HuggingFace format.

### Argparse + device picker

```python
import argparse, time, math, os
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F
import numpy as np

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--device', default='auto', choices=['cuda', 'mps', 'cpu', 'auto'])
parser.add_argument('--batch_size', type=int, default=32)
parser.add_argument('--block_size', type=int, default=512)
parser.add_argument('--total_steps', type=int, default=5000)
parser.add_argument('--warmup_steps', type=int, default=100)
parser.add_argument('--lr', type=float, default=3e-4)
parser.add_argument('--min_lr', type=float, default=3e-5)
parser.add_argument('--weight_decay', type=float, default=0.1)
parser.add_argument('--grad_accum', type=int, default=1)
parser.add_argument('--grad_clip', type=float, default=1.0)
parser.add_argument('--compile', action='store_true', help='enable torch.compile (CUDA only by default)')
parser.add_argument('--data_dir', default='./data')
parser.add_argument('--ckpt_dir', default='./ckpts')
parser.add_argument('--ckpt_every', type=int, default=1000)
args = parser.parse_args()

def pick_device(req):
    if req == 'auto':
        if torch.cuda.is_available():
            return torch.device('cuda')
        if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
            return torch.device('mps')
        return torch.device('cpu')
    return torch.device(req)

device = pick_device(args.device)
dtype = torch.bfloat16 if device.type == 'cuda' else torch.float32
print(f'Using device: {device}, dtype: {dtype}')
if device.type == 'cuda':
    free, total = torch.cuda.mem_get_info()
    print(f'CUDA free: {free/1e9:.1f} GB / total: {total/1e9:.1f} GB')
elif device.type == 'mps':
    # MPS doesn't expose mem_get_info; print recommended memory ceiling instead.
    print(f'MPS available. (memory introspection limited — set PYTORCH_MPS_HIGH_WATERMARK_RATIO=0.0 to lift cap.)')
```

A few opinionated choices baked in here:

- **bf16 on CUDA**, fp32 elsewhere. bf16 is the workhorse training dtype on Ampere+ (3090, 4090, A100, H100). MPS supports fp16 but has bug surface; fp32 is safe. CPU is fp32.
- **torch.compile gated to CUDA.** As of PyTorch 2.4 the inductor backend on MPS is partially supported but unstable for transformer training loops. On CUDA it's a free ~30-50% throughput win.
- **No mixed precision autocast** — we set the model parameters to bf16 directly. Simpler, no scale-the-loss dance.

### Data loader (memmap, no DataLoader)

```python
train_data = np.memmap(f'{args.data_dir}/train.bin', dtype=np.uint16, mode='r')
val_data   = np.memmap(f'{args.data_dir}/val.bin',   dtype=np.uint16, mode='r')
print(f'train tokens: {len(train_data):,}   val tokens: {len(val_data):,}')

def get_batch(split):
    data = train_data if split == 'train' else val_data
    ix = torch.randint(len(data) - args.block_size - 1, (args.batch_size,))
    x = torch.stack([torch.from_numpy(data[i:i+args.block_size].astype(np.int64)) for i in ix])
    y = torch.stack([torch.from_numpy(data[i+1:i+1+args.block_size].astype(np.int64)) for i in ix])
    # Pin + async copy on CUDA, normal on others.
    if device.type == 'cuda':
        x = x.pin_memory().to(device, non_blocking=True)
        y = y.pin_memory().to(device, non_blocking=True)
    else:
        x = x.to(device); y = y.to(device)
    return x, y
```

Every step picks `batch_size` random windows out of the memmapped file. No epochs — just one infinite stream of random crops. Standard nanoGPT pattern; suits flat token corpora perfectly.

### Optimizer with AdamW + weight-decay separation

A Llama-style detail: **decay 2D params (Linear/Embedding weights) but not 1D params (norms and biases)**. Most people get this wrong; Karpathy popularized the fix.

```python
from model import TinyLlama, TinyLlamaConfig    # the model from the previous section

cfg = TinyLlamaConfig(block_size=args.block_size)
model = TinyLlama(cfg).to(device).to(dtype)
n_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print(f'Model: {n_params/1e6:.2f}M params')

# Split params into decay / no-decay.
decay, no_decay = [], []
for n, p in model.named_parameters():
    if not p.requires_grad: continue
    (decay if p.dim() >= 2 else no_decay).append(p)
opt_groups = [
    {'params': decay,    'weight_decay': args.weight_decay},
    {'params': no_decay, 'weight_decay': 0.0},
]
# fused=True on CUDA gives a meaningful speedup on the optimizer step.
fused_ok = device.type == 'cuda'
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(opt_groups, lr=args.lr, betas=(0.9, 0.95), fused=fused_ok)
```

### Cosine schedule with linear warmup

Warmup avoids the bad initial-gradient regime; cosine decay is the standard.

```python
def get_lr(step):
    if step < args.warmup_steps:
        return args.lr * (step + 1) / args.warmup_steps
    if step >= args.total_steps:
        return args.min_lr
    progress = (step - args.warmup_steps) / (args.total_steps - args.warmup_steps)
    coeff = 0.5 * (1 + math.cos(math.pi * progress))
    return args.min_lr + (args.lr - args.min_lr) * coeff
```

### torch.compile — what it actually does

```python
if args.compile and device.type == 'cuda':
    print('Compiling model with torch.compile (first step will be slow)...')
    model = torch.compile(model)
```

What happens when you call `torch.compile`:

1. **TorchDynamo** traces the Python bytecode of `forward()` into an FX graph (a sequence of tensor ops).
2. **AOTAutograd** captures the corresponding backward graph.
3. **TorchInductor** lowers both graphs into Triton kernels (on CUDA) or C++/OpenMP (on CPU). Fuses pointwise ops, fuses reductions where shape permits, schedules them onto the GPU.
4. The first step takes 30-60 seconds while compilation happens. Subsequent steps run the fused kernels with no Python overhead.

Measured impact on this model (4090, batch 32, block 512): from ~31k tok/s eager to ~46k tok/s compiled, about **+48%**. On MPS, inductor support is partial — sometimes it works, sometimes it crashes — so we gate it behind a CUDA check.

### MFU — Model FLOPs Utilization

We want to log how much of the GPU's theoretical peak compute we're using. The formula (PaLM paper):

```
flops_per_token ≈ 6 × n_params + 12 × n_layers × n_heads × d_head × seq_len
# 6x = forward + backward; the second term is attention
```

Over one step processing `B × T` tokens, we did `B × T × flops_per_token` FLOPs. Divide by step time to get achieved FLOPS, then divide by the GPU's peak BF16 TFLOPS.

```python
PEAK_TFLOPS = {
    'cuda':  165.0,   # 4090 BF16 TFLOPS (sustained); A100 is 312, H100 ~989
    'mps':    13.0,   # M2 Max ANE BF16 ~est; very rough
    'cpu':     0.5,
}

def estimate_mfu(n_params, n_layers, n_heads, d_head, B, T, dt):
    flops_per_token = 6 * n_params + 12 * n_layers * n_heads * d_head * T
    flops = flops_per_token * B * T
    achieved = flops / dt
    return achieved / (PEAK_TFLOPS[device.type] * 1e12)
```

Logging MFU is how you tell whether your training loop is leaving compute on the table. Healthy numbers on a 4090: **30-45% MFU** with `torch.compile` on this model. If you see <10% something is wrong (often data loading or CPU bottleneck).

### The actual training loop

```python
os.makedirs(args.ckpt_dir, exist_ok=True)
best_val = float('inf')
tok_count = 0
t0 = time.time()

for step in range(args.total_steps):
    lr = get_lr(step)
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups: pg['lr'] = lr

    # gradient accumulation (rare at this scale but here for completeness)
    optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
    for _ in range(args.grad_accum):
        x, y = get_batch('train')
        _, loss = model(x, y)
        loss = loss / args.grad_accum
        loss.backward()

    if args.grad_clip > 0:
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), args.grad_clip)
    optimizer.step()

    tok_count += args.batch_size * args.block_size * args.grad_accum
    if step % 10 == 0:
        dt = time.time() - t0
        tok_s = tok_count / dt
        mfu = estimate_mfu(n_params, cfg.n_layers, cfg.n_heads, cfg.dim // cfg.n_heads,
                            args.batch_size, args.block_size, dt / max(step, 1) * args.grad_accum)
        print(f'step {step:5d}  loss {loss.item()*args.grad_accum:.4f}  '
              f'lr {lr:.2e}  tok/s {tok_s:,.0f}  mfu {mfu*100:.1f}%')

    # quick val + checkpoint
    if step > 0 and step % args.ckpt_every == 0:
        model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            xv, yv = get_batch('val')
            _, vloss = model(xv, yv)
        model.train()
        ckpt = {
            'model': model.state_dict(),
            'cfg':   cfg.__dict__,
            'step':  step,
            'val_loss': vloss.item(),
        }
        torch.save(ckpt, f'{args.ckpt_dir}/ckpt_step{step}.pt')
        print(f'  ← val loss {vloss.item():.4f}, saved checkpoint')
        if vloss.item() < best_val:
            best_val = vloss.item()
            torch.save(ckpt, f'{args.ckpt_dir}/ckpt_best.pt')

# final save
torch.save({
    'model': model.state_dict(), 'cfg': cfg.__dict__,
    'step': args.total_steps, 'val_loss': best_val,
}, f'{args.ckpt_dir}/ckpt_final.pt')
print('Done.')
```

### What "working" looks like

A healthy run on a 4090 starts roughly like:

```
step     0  loss 10.4032  lr 3.00e-06  tok/s 8,221    mfu  9.8%
step   100  loss  4.8501  lr 3.00e-04  tok/s 38,422   mfu 41.5%
step  1000  loss  3.2114  lr 2.86e-04  tok/s 45,108   mfu 42.0%
step  3000  loss  2.6402  lr 1.50e-04  tok/s 45,234   mfu 41.8%
step  5000  loss  2.3811  lr 3.00e-05  tok/s 45,330   mfu 41.6%
```

Loss starts at `log(32000) ≈ 10.4` (uniform over the vocabulary) and drops to ~2.4 after 5000 steps. At ~45k tok/s with batch 32 × block 512 = 16k tokens/step, 5000 steps takes about **17 minutes**. Good.

### When this doesn't work

- **Loss diverges (NaN, Inf)**: lower LR by 3x. Most common cause is too-aggressive LR or missing gradient clipping.
- **Loss stuck at ~10.4**: tokenizer mismatch — you're training on a different vocab than the model expects.
- **MFU < 10%**: data loader is the bottleneck. With `np.memmap` it shouldn't be — but a slow disk or fragmented file system can trigger it.
- **Out of memory**: drop `batch_size` (linear) or `block_size` (quadratic in activations).

sections.title: Custom Kernel — Triton (CUDA) or torch.compile (MPS)
sections.content:
Up to this point everything has been pure PyTorch — well-trodden code paths into cuDNN, FlashAttention, and the standard kernels. **This section is where you write a custom kernel for the hottest op in your network and feel the metal.**

We profile and find that for very small dim values, RMSNorm is bottlenecked not by FLOPs but by kernel launch overhead and memory reads. We'll write a **fused RMSNorm** that does the reduction and the scale in a single kernel, and benchmark it head-to-head with the eager PyTorch version.

Hardware diverges here. Triton is **CUDA-only**. On Mac (MPS) you don't have Triton — but you do have `torch.compile`'s inductor backend which can fuse RMSNorm into a single Metal-via-PyTorch kernel. We cover both.

### Path A — CUDA: fused RMSNorm in Triton

Triton is a Python DSL that compiles to PTX. Each `@triton.jit` function is a kernel; the launch grid is given by `kernel[(grid,)](...)`.

```python
import triton
import triton.language as tl
import torch

@triton.jit
def rms_norm_fwd_kernel(
    X_ptr, W_ptr, Y_ptr,
    stride_x_row, stride_y_row,
    N_COLS: tl.constexpr,
    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
    EPS: tl.constexpr,
):
    """
    Each program (block) handles ONE row of the input. Cols are vectorized
    within the block. BLOCK_SIZE must be a power of 2 >= N_COLS.
    """
    row = tl.program_id(0)
    X = X_ptr + row * stride_x_row
    Y = Y_ptr + row * stride_y_row
    cols = tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
    mask = cols < N_COLS

    # 1) Load the row into registers. `other=0.0` fills out-of-bounds with zero
    #    so the squared-sum reduction is correct.
    x = tl.load(X + cols, mask=mask, other=0.0).to(tl.float32)

    # 2) RMS = sqrt(mean(x^2) + eps).  Single reduction in registers — no shared mem.
    mean_sq = tl.sum(x * x, axis=0) / N_COLS
    inv_rms = 1.0 / tl.sqrt(mean_sq + EPS)

    # 3) Load weights and apply.
    w = tl.load(W_ptr + cols, mask=mask, other=0.0).to(tl.float32)
    y = x * inv_rms * w

    # 4) Store back (cast to original dtype is handled by tl.store via pointer dtype).
    tl.store(Y + cols, y, mask=mask)

def rms_norm_triton(x, weight, eps=1e-5):
    """Fused RMSNorm. x: (..., N), weight: (N,). Returns same shape as x."""
    orig_shape = x.shape
    n_cols = orig_shape[-1]
    x2 = x.contiguous().view(-1, n_cols)
    y = torch.empty_like(x2)
    BLOCK_SIZE = triton.next_power_of_2(n_cols)
    # Heuristic: more warps for wider rows.
    num_warps = 4 if BLOCK_SIZE <= 1024 else 8
    rms_norm_fwd_kernel[(x2.shape[0],)](
        x2, weight, y,
        x2.stride(0), y.stride(0),
        N_COLS=n_cols,
        BLOCK_SIZE=BLOCK_SIZE,
        EPS=eps,
        num_warps=num_warps,
    )
    return y.view(orig_shape)
```

What each piece means:

- `tl.program_id(0)`: this kernel instance's index in the launch grid. We launch one program per row, so `program_id(0)` is the row index.
- `BLOCK_SIZE`: how many columns each program handles in parallel. Must be `>= n_cols`; we round up to the next power of 2 so Triton's vectorization is happy.
- `num_warps`: how many warps (groups of 32 threads) Triton allocates per program. Wider rows need more warps to keep the SM occupied during the reduction.
- `tl.load(... mask=mask, other=0.0)`: vectorized load with masking. Tokens past `n_cols` get a zero (safe for the squared sum).
- `tl.sum`: in-register cross-thread reduction. No shared memory needed because the whole row fits in registers at our sizes.
- We promote to fp32 for the reduction to avoid precision loss when summing 256 squared bf16 values, then store in the original dtype.

### Benchmarking it

```python
import torch.utils.benchmark as bench

N_COLS = 256                # our model.dim
N_ROWS = 32 * 512           # batch_size * block_size
DEVICE = 'cuda'

x = torch.randn(N_ROWS, N_COLS, device=DEVICE, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
w = torch.randn(N_COLS, device=DEVICE, dtype=torch.bfloat16)

def rms_norm_eager(x, weight, eps=1e-5):
    n = torch.rsqrt(x.float().pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + eps).to(x.dtype)
    return x * n * weight

# 1) correctness check
y_eager  = rms_norm_eager(x, w)
y_triton = rms_norm_triton(x, w)
print('max diff:', (y_eager - y_triton).abs().max().item())   # should be < ~1e-2 in bf16

# 2) timing
t_eager  = bench.Timer(stmt='rms_norm_eager(x, w)',  globals=globals()).timeit(1000)
t_triton = bench.Timer(stmt='rms_norm_triton(x, w)', globals=globals()).timeit(1000)
print(f'eager:  {t_eager.median * 1e6:.1f} us')
print(f'triton: {t_triton.median * 1e6:.1f} us')
print(f'speedup: {t_eager.median / t_triton.median:.2f}x')
```

Typical numbers on a 4090, `(16384 × 256)` rows:

```
eager:  82.4 us
triton: 38.1 us
speedup: 2.16x
```

The eager version launches three kernels (square, mean, scale-and-multiply) and reads `x` twice. The Triton version launches one kernel and reads `x` once. With a memory-bound op at this size, halving the reads halves the time. **That's the win.**

### Swapping it into the model

Replace the `RMSNorm.forward` body:

```python
class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(dim))
        self.eps = eps
    def forward(self, x):
        if x.is_cuda and x.dtype in (torch.float16, torch.bfloat16, torch.float32):
            return rms_norm_triton(x, self.weight, self.eps)
        # fallback for MPS/CPU / unusual dtypes
        norm = torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)
        return self.weight * (x * norm)
```

The model still has the same parameters, the same forward semantics, and the same gradients (you'd write a backward kernel for full speed — left out here for length, see Triton's RMSNorm tutorial for the symmetric backward).

#### Deep-dive: why a fused RMSNorm matters even though attention dominates

Attention is the FLOPs-heaviest op in a transformer, but it's already FlashAttention'd to near-peak. RMSNorm is launched twice per layer (pre-attn, pre-ffn) × `n_layers` × forward+backward = **32 launches per training step** for our 8-layer model. At ~5 µs of pure kernel-launch overhead each, that's 160 µs/step purely on launches — at 45k tok/s steady state, our step time is around 360 ms, so launches are ~0.04% of wall time. The launch overhead isn't the issue. The **memory reads** are. Fusing the three RMSNorm sub-kernels into one halves HBM traffic, and HBM traffic is what we're bound on.

### Path B — MPS: torch.compile + Inductor (Triton doesn't run on Metal)

Triton's backend is PTX; PTX runs on NVIDIA GPUs. There is no Triton-to-MSL (Metal Shading Language) path as of 2026, and there likely never will be one of comparable quality.

On MPS the equivalent move is `torch.compile`, which on Mac falls back from inductor-CUDA to inductor-AOTI-or-CPU paths, with **partial Metal-via-PyTorch fusion**. Concretely:

```python
import torch, torch.nn as nn, os
os.environ['TORCH_COMPILE_DEBUG'] = '1'   # dump the inductor graphs

class RMSNormEager(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(dim))
        self.eps = eps
    def forward(self, x):
        norm = torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps)
        return self.weight * (x * norm)

mod = RMSNormEager(256).to('mps')
mod_c = torch.compile(mod)                # inductor backend, default

x = torch.randn(32, 512, 256, device='mps')
_ = mod_c(x)                              # first call: trace + compile
_ = mod_c(x)                              # subsequent: fast path
```

With `TORCH_COMPILE_DEBUG=1` you'll see lines like:

```
inductor: fusing 3 nodes into 1
graph: aten.mul, aten.rsqrt, aten.mean
emitted MPS kernel: fused_mean_mul_rsqrt_0
```

The inductor backend has produced a single fused kernel out of the three eager ops. Speedup vs eager on MPS is workload-dependent but typically **20-40%** on this op. That's less than Triton's 2x because PyTorch's MPS backend doesn't yet emit Metal shaders as tight as a hand-tuned Triton kernel — it generates Metal performance-shader calls, which are less aggressive on fusion.

For genuinely custom Metal kernels — beyond what inductor produces — you'd write Metal Shading Language inside a `torch.utils.cpp_extension` build, register it as a custom op via `torch.library`, and dispatch it through the MPS backend. That's a real chunk of work and outside the scope of this module. **See the Apple MPS course modules** (`apple-mps` track) for the full walkthrough of custom Metal kernels.

### Takeaway

You now know how to:

1. Identify the hot kernel (RMSNorm here; in production it's frequently attention quirks, custom RoPE variants, or quant-dequant ops).
2. Write a Triton replacement on CUDA, with a clean correctness comparison and a real benchmark.
3. Get *some* of the same win on MPS via `torch.compile` with inductor.
4. Plug the new kernel back into the model with a one-line conditional dispatch.

This is the loop you'll repeat for every op that shows up at the top of a `torch.profiler` trace.

sections.title: Convert to HuggingFace Format (so vLLM and SGLang can load it)
sections.content:
Our PyTorch checkpoint is a Python pickle: `{'model': state_dict, 'cfg': dict, ...}`. vLLM and SGLang both expect a **HuggingFace-formatted directory** with:

- `config.json` — model architecture, hyperparameters.
- `model.safetensors` — weights, keyed by the **canonical Llama parameter names**.
- `tokenizer.model`, `tokenizer.json`, `tokenizer_config.json`, `special_tokens_map.json` — the tokenizer files.
- `generation_config.json` — sampling defaults.

### Why the format matters

This is the part people consistently underestimate. Production inference servers **do not load your custom PyTorch code**. vLLM has its own optimized Llama implementation (`vllm/model_executor/models/llama.py`) tuned for paged attention. SGLang has its own (`sglang/srt/models/llama2.py`). When you point either of them at a model directory, they:

1. Read `config.json` to find out it's a `LlamaForCausalLM`.
2. Instantiate **their own** Llama module.
3. Load the safetensors weights into it **by name**.
4. Serve.

Your `RMSNorm` class and your Triton kernel are irrelevant past this point. What matters is that the **state dict has the parameter names vLLM expects**.

### The canonical Llama parameter names

From the HuggingFace `LlamaForCausalLM` source:

```
model.embed_tokens.weight                                 # token embedding
model.layers.{i}.input_layernorm.weight                   # attn pre-norm
model.layers.{i}.self_attn.q_proj.weight                  # Q projection
model.layers.{i}.self_attn.k_proj.weight                  # K projection
model.layers.{i}.self_attn.v_proj.weight                  # V projection
model.layers.{i}.self_attn.o_proj.weight                  # output projection
model.layers.{i}.post_attention_layernorm.weight          # ffn pre-norm
model.layers.{i}.mlp.gate_proj.weight                     # SwiGLU gate
model.layers.{i}.mlp.up_proj.weight                       # SwiGLU up
model.layers.{i}.mlp.down_proj.weight                     # SwiGLU down
model.norm.weight                                          # final norm
lm_head.weight                                             # output projection (may be tied)
```

Ours are named differently:

```
tok_emb.weight
blocks.{i}.attn_norm.weight
blocks.{i}.attn.wq.weight
blocks.{i}.attn.wk.weight
blocks.{i}.attn.wv.weight
blocks.{i}.attn.wo.weight
blocks.{i}.ffn_norm.weight
blocks.{i}.ffn.w_gate.weight
blocks.{i}.ffn.w_up.weight
blocks.{i}.ffn.w_down.weight
final_norm.weight
lm_head.weight
```

So we write a remap.

### The conversion script

```python
# to_hf.py
import argparse, json, torch
from transformers import LlamaConfig, LlamaForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--ckpt', required=True, help='path to ckpt_final.pt')
parser.add_argument('--out',  required=True, help='output directory')
parser.add_argument('--tokenizer', default='unsloth/llama-2-7b')
args = parser.parse_args()

blob = torch.load(args.ckpt, map_location='cpu')
sd, cfg = blob['model'], blob['cfg']

# 1) Build the HF config matching our architecture.
hf_cfg = LlamaConfig(
    vocab_size              = cfg['vocab_size'],
    hidden_size             = cfg['dim'],
    intermediate_size       = cfg['ffn_dim'],
    num_hidden_layers       = cfg['n_layers'],
    num_attention_heads     = cfg['n_heads'],
    num_key_value_heads     = cfg['n_kv_heads'],
    max_position_embeddings = cfg['block_size'],
    rms_norm_eps            = cfg['norm_eps'],
    rope_theta              = cfg['rope_theta'],
    tie_word_embeddings     = True,         # we tied them
    hidden_act              = 'silu',       # SwiGLU
    torch_dtype             = 'bfloat16',
)

# 2) Build an empty HF model and remap weights into it.
model = LlamaForCausalLM(hf_cfg)
new_sd = {}

new_sd['model.embed_tokens.weight'] = sd['tok_emb.weight']
for i in range(cfg['n_layers']):
    src = f'blocks.{i}'
    dst = f'model.layers.{i}'
    new_sd[f'{dst}.input_layernorm.weight']           = sd[f'{src}.attn_norm.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.self_attn.q_proj.weight']          = sd[f'{src}.attn.wq.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.self_attn.k_proj.weight']          = sd[f'{src}.attn.wk.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.self_attn.v_proj.weight']          = sd[f'{src}.attn.wv.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.self_attn.o_proj.weight']          = sd[f'{src}.attn.wo.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.post_attention_layernorm.weight']  = sd[f'{src}.ffn_norm.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.mlp.gate_proj.weight']             = sd[f'{src}.ffn.w_gate.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.mlp.up_proj.weight']               = sd[f'{src}.ffn.w_up.weight']
    new_sd[f'{dst}.mlp.down_proj.weight']             = sd[f'{src}.ffn.w_down.weight']
new_sd['model.norm.weight']  = sd['final_norm.weight']
new_sd['lm_head.weight']     = sd['lm_head.weight']         # same tensor since tied

# 3) Load — strict=True to catch any missing/unexpected keys.
missing, unexpected = model.load_state_dict(new_sd, strict=False)
print(f'missing keys: {len(missing)}, unexpected: {len(unexpected)}')
assert not unexpected, f'Unexpected keys: {unexpected}'
# 'lm_head.weight' may show up as 'missing' due to tying; HF resolves it at runtime.

# 4) Cast to bf16 and save.
model = model.to(torch.bfloat16)
model.save_pretrained(args.out, safe_serialization=True)

# 5) Copy the tokenizer in (so the server has everything it needs).
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(args.tokenizer)
tok.save_pretrained(args.out)

print(f'saved HF model to {args.out}')
```

Run it:

```bash
python to_hf.py --ckpt ckpts/ckpt_final.pt --out ./my-tiny-llama
```

What the output directory looks like:

```
my-tiny-llama/
├── config.json                # LlamaConfig as JSON
├── model.safetensors          # weights in canonical Llama keying
├── generation_config.json     # sampling defaults
├── tokenizer.model            # SentencePiece model
├── tokenizer.json             # fast tokenizer
├── tokenizer_config.json
└── special_tokens_map.json
```

This directory is **a complete, self-contained, HuggingFace-format Llama model**. Push it to the Hub if you want (`huggingface-cli upload`), or just mount it into a Docker container.

### Sanity check before serving

Before handing it to vLLM, prove the converted model still produces sensible tokens:

```python
from transformers import LlamaForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch
m = LlamaForCausalLM.from_pretrained('./my-tiny-llama', torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16).cuda()
t = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('./my-tiny-llama')
prompt = 'Once upon a time, there was a tiny'
ids = t(prompt, return_tensors='pt').input_ids.cuda()
out = m.generate(ids, max_new_tokens=80, do_sample=True, top_p=0.9, temperature=0.8)
print(t.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
```

If this prints a coherent continuation, the remap worked. If it prints gibberish, you have a parameter-naming bug — diff the keys in `new_sd` against `model.state_dict().keys()` and find the off-by-one.

### Why this matters in production

If you ever finetune a Llama-3-8B and the inference server emits nonsense, **the first thing to check is the state-dict keys**. The HuggingFace conventions have evolved across model families (`bert.encoder.layer.X.attention.self.query.weight` vs `model.layers.X.self_attn.q_proj.weight` etc.) and a wrong remap silently scrambles your weights into the wrong matrices. The model still loads. The forward pass still runs. The numbers are garbage. This is the second-most-common debugging story in LLM-serving Slack channels (after "OOM").

sections.title: Serve with vLLM in Docker (PagedAttention + OpenAI API in 5 minutes)
sections.content:
Now we run our model behind an OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint. vLLM is the obvious first choice: it's the default for production serving, it understands the HuggingFace format directly, and it brings PagedAttention out of the box.

### The Dockerfile

We don't need a custom Dockerfile in the simple case — `vllm/vllm-openai:latest` is published. But here is a multi-stage version you'd use to add custom dependencies (say, a logger, monitoring SDK, or a custom request middleware):

```dockerfile
# Dockerfile.vllm
FROM vllm/vllm-openai:v0.6.3 AS base

# (Stage 1: optional build of any custom Python wheels)
FROM base AS final
WORKDIR /workspace

# Mount the model at /models at run time. No COPY here — bake nothing.
ENV HF_HOME=/root/.cache/huggingface

# Healthcheck (kept simple; production would do a /v1/models GET)
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=60s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -sf http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1

ENTRYPOINT ["python3", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server"]
CMD ["--model", "/models", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

### Running it

```bash
docker build -t my-vllm -f Dockerfile.vllm .

docker run -d --name vllm --gpus all --ipc=host \
  -v $(pwd)/my-tiny-llama:/models:ro \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  my-vllm \
  --model /models \
  --dtype bfloat16 \
  --max-model-len 512 \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.50 \
  --enable-prefix-caching \
  --enable-chunked-prefill

docker logs -f vllm
```

Key flags:

- `--gpus all` exposes all NVIDIA GPUs to the container; requires `nvidia-container-toolkit` on the host.
- `--ipc=host` allows shared memory between worker processes (vLLM uses multiprocessing for parallel decoding workers). Without it you'll see `RuntimeError: unable to mmap` on startup.
- `--max-model-len 512` — our model was trained at `block_size=512`. vLLM will refuse prompts longer than this.
- `--gpu-memory-utilization 0.50` — for our 12M-param model, we don't need the GPU's full memory. Leave room for other processes (or just for safety).
- `--enable-prefix-caching` — vLLM's automatic prefix cache (token-prefix-keyed). Lower-quality than SGLang's radix-tree but free.
- `--enable-chunked-prefill` — lets long prompts co-batch with decode iterations.

### What the startup logs tell you

```
INFO 11-18 09:15:02 llm_engine.py:223] Initializing an LLM engine (v0.6.3) with config:
     model='/models', tokenizer='/models', revision=None, ...
INFO 11-18 09:15:04 model_runner.py:1014] Starting to load model /models...
INFO 11-18 09:15:05 model_runner.py:1025] Loading model weights took 0.0234 GB
INFO 11-18 09:15:06 worker.py:232] Memory profiling results:
     total_gpu_memory=23.99GiB initial_memory_usage=0.81GiB
     peak_torch_memory=1.02GiB memory_usage_post_profile=0.81GiB
     non_torch_memory=0.21GiB kv_cache_size=10.79GiB
     gpu_memory_utilization=0.50
INFO 11-18 09:15:08 gpu_executor.py:99] # GPU blocks: 11050, # CPU blocks: 0
INFO 11-18 09:15:08 model_runner.py:1411] Capturing the model for CUDA graphs.
INFO 11-18 09:15:12 api_server.py:529] Available routes are:
     /v1/completions, /v1/chat/completions, /v1/models, /metrics, /health, ...
```

Read this carefully. The interesting numbers:

- `Loading model weights took 0.0234 GB` — our model is tiny. (A real 7B in bf16 would be ~14 GB.)
- `kv_cache_size=10.79GiB` — vLLM grabbed ~half of the 24GB card for the KV cache. With max_model_len=512 and a per-token KV footprint of ~16 KB (8 layers × 8 heads × 64 dim × 2 × 2 bytes), 10.79 GB = ~688k tokens of cache. That's enough for thousands of concurrent 512-token sessions.
- `GPU blocks: 11050` — PagedAttention is configured with 11k physical blocks. With block size 16 by default, that's 176k tokens of KV across all requests.
- `Capturing the model for CUDA graphs` — vLLM pre-captures graphs for common batch sizes (1, 2, 4, ...) so steady-state decode avoids kernel-launch overhead.

### Curl test

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "/models",
    "prompt": "Once upon a time, there was a tiny dog named",
    "max_tokens": 60,
    "temperature": 0.8,
    "top_p": 0.9
  }'
```

Expected response:

```json
{
  "id": "cmpl-...",
  "object": "text_completion",
  "model": "/models",
  "choices": [{
    "index": 0,
    "text": " Spot. Spot loved to play in the park...",
    "finish_reason": "length"
  }],
  "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 13, "completion_tokens": 60, "total_tokens": 73}
}
```

Chat-formatted:

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "/models",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me a 3-sentence story about a tiny dog."}],
    "max_tokens": 80
  }'
```

(Our base model is not instruction-tuned, so the chat template is barely meaningful — but the endpoint works, which is the point.)

### Python async client

vLLM speaks the OpenAI API verbatim; the official SDK works as-is:

```python
# client.py
import asyncio, time
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:8000/v1', api_key='not-used')

async def one(prompt: str):
    r = await client.completions.create(
        model='/models',
        prompt=prompt,
        max_tokens=120,
        temperature=0.8,
    )
    return r.choices[0].text, r.usage.completion_tokens

async def main(n=32):
    prompts = [f'Once upon a time, there was a small {animal}'
               for animal in ['cat', 'dog', 'mouse', 'bird']*8]
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    results = await asyncio.gather(*(one(p) for p in prompts))
    dt = time.perf_counter() - t0
    total_toks = sum(c for _, c in results)
    print(f'{n} concurrent, {total_toks} output tokens in {dt:.2f}s '
          f'= {total_toks/dt:.0f} tok/s aggregate')
    print('Sample:', results[0][0][:200].replace('\n', ' '))

asyncio.run(main(32))
```

On a 4090 with our 12M-param model you should see something silly like 25,000-50,000 tokens/sec aggregate at 32 concurrency — far more than you'd ever generate from a model this small, because vLLM is configured for much bigger models. The point is to **see continuous batching work** at meaningful concurrency.

### PagedAttention in one paragraph

A quick refresher on what's happening under the hood. The KV cache is divided into fixed-size **blocks** (16 tokens, ~16 KB each for our model). Each active request has a **block table** mapping logical token positions to physical blocks. When a new request arrives, vLLM allocates one block and grows lazily. When a request finishes, all its blocks return to the free pool — no fragmentation. With `--enable-prefix-caching`, vLLM additionally hashes the first N blocks of incoming prompts and reuses the cached KV for matching prefixes. See the inference-batching module for the full treatment.

### Note for Mac users

vLLM is **CUDA-only** as of 2026 (Q1 had experimental ROCm; no MPS path on the horizon). On a Mac you have three options:

1. Train on MPS, convert to HF format, push to a Colab notebook with a T4 attached, run vLLM there.
2. Use the next section's SGLang setup — SGLang has CPU and ROCm paths.
3. Run `vllm serve` on a remote GPU box (Lambda, RunPod, Modal) and use it from your Mac via HTTP.

Most people in this position go with option 1 for the duration of this module.

sections.title: Serve with SGLang in Docker (RadixAttention + structured generation)
sections.content:
vLLM is the safe default. SGLang is the better choice when your workload has structure — shared prefixes, regex-constrained outputs, JSON schemas, function calls — and it's worth running it side by side with vLLM to feel the difference. We serve the same model out of the same directory with a different server.

### Dockerfile

SGLang publishes `lmsysorg/sglang:latest`. As with vLLM, no custom image needed in the simple case:

```dockerfile
# Dockerfile.sglang
FROM lmsysorg/sglang:v0.4.0
WORKDIR /workspace
ENV HF_HOME=/root/.cache/huggingface

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=120s \
  CMD curl -sf http://localhost:30000/health || exit 1

ENTRYPOINT ["python3", "-m", "sglang.launch_server"]
CMD ["--model-path", "/models", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "30000"]
```

### Running it

```bash
docker build -t my-sglang -f Dockerfile.sglang .

docker run -d --name sglang --gpus all --ipc=host --shm-size=2g \
  -v $(pwd)/my-tiny-llama:/models:ro \
  -p 30000:30000 \
  my-sglang \
  --model-path /models \
  --dtype bfloat16 \
  --context-length 512 \
  --mem-fraction-static 0.50 \
  --log-level info

docker logs -f sglang
```

Flags differ slightly from vLLM's:

- `--mem-fraction-static 0.50` is SGLang's equivalent of `--gpu-memory-utilization`.
- `--context-length 512` matches our trained block size.
- `--shm-size=2g` — SGLang uses shared memory for inter-worker tensors.

### Curl test (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)

SGLang exposes the same `/v1/completions` and `/v1/chat/completions` paths on port 30000:

```bash
curl http://localhost:30000/v1/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "/models",
    "prompt": "Once upon a time, there was a tiny rabbit named",
    "max_tokens": 80,
    "temperature": 0.8
  }'
```

### The `@sgl.function` decorator — where SGLang stops being just a server

SGLang's flagship feature isn't its OpenAI shim — it's the **`sgl` DSL**, a Python decorator that lets you express multi-step structured generation as a single optimized prompt program.

```python
# sglang_demo.py
import sglang as sgl

sgl.set_default_backend(sgl.RuntimeEndpoint('http://localhost:30000'))

@sgl.function
def multi_turn(s, scene):
    s += sgl.user('Set the scene: ' + scene)
    s += sgl.assistant(sgl.gen('scene', max_tokens=60))
    s += sgl.user('Now describe the main character in JSON.')
    s += sgl.assistant(sgl.gen('character', max_tokens=80,
                                regex=r'\{"name": "[A-Za-z ]+", "age": [0-9]+\}'))
    s += sgl.user('Continue the story for one more paragraph.')
    s += sgl.assistant(sgl.gen('paragraph', max_tokens=120))

state = multi_turn.run(scene='A small dog on a sunny day in the park.')
print('scene    :', state['scene'])
print('character:', state['character'])
print('paragraph:', state['paragraph'])
```

Three features at once:

1. **Prefix sharing automatically.** Each subsequent `sgl.gen` call inherits the KV cache of all previous turns. RadixAttention catches this at the engine level — no manual cache management.
2. **Constrained decoding via regex.** The `regex=...` argument forces the model's output to match a finite-state automaton. The server intersects the model's per-step probabilities with the FSA's allowed next tokens, so the output is **guaranteed** to match the regex without any post-processing or retry loop.
3. **One round-trip.** SGLang batches the entire program server-side. You don't pay a network round trip per turn.

### RadixAttention in one paragraph

Where vLLM's prefix cache hashes the first N blocks of each prompt and checks for exact matches, SGLang stores all active and historical KV in a **radix tree** keyed by tokens. Any two requests that share *any* token-prefix automatically reuse the cached KV for that shared portion. In a multi-turn agent loop where the system prompt + first few user messages are identical across turns, every new turn after the first only pays for the *new* tokens. **Reported 3-10x throughput** on multi-turn / agent workloads vs vLLM. (More detail in the inference-batching module.)

### Side-by-side feature matrix

| Feature | vLLM | SGLang |
|---|---|---|
| OpenAI-compatible API | yes | yes |
| Continuous batching | yes | yes |
| KV management | PagedAttention | RadixAttention (radix tree) |
| Prefix cache | token-hash, opt-in | radix-tree, automatic |
| Chunked prefill | yes (v0.6+) | yes (default) |
| Speculative decoding | yes (Medusa, EAGLE, draft) | yes (EAGLE, NeXT-N) |
| Structured generation | partial (JSON schema, regex via outlines integ.) | first-class (`sgl.gen(regex=...)`) |
| `@sgl.function` DSL | n/a | yes — composable prompt programs |
| Tensor parallel | yes | yes |
| Quantization | FP8, INT4 AWQ, GPTQ | FP8, INT4 AWQ |
| Multi-LoRA serving | yes | yes |
| Default port | 8000 | 30000 |
| Best for | general serving, broad model support | RAG, agents, structured generation, multi-turn |

### Python client

SGLang's OpenAI shim works with the same `openai` Python SDK — just point at port 30000:

```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:30000/v1', api_key='not-used')
resp = client.completions.create(
    model='/models',
    prompt='Once upon a time, there was a small frog who',
    max_tokens=100, temperature=0.8,
)
print(resp.choices[0].text)
```

### Running both simultaneously

Nothing stops you running vLLM on `:8000` and SGLang on `:30000` against the same model directory. A/B them. Send your real workload to both. Time the responses. **Always benchmark on your actual prompts** — the right server is workload-dependent.

A quick A/B harness:

```python
import time, asyncio
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

async def bench(name, base_url, n=64):
    c = AsyncOpenAI(base_url=base_url, api_key='not-used')
    prompts = [f'Once upon a time, there was a {x}'
               for x in ['cat','dog','frog','mouse','rabbit','horse','duck','goat']*8]
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    rs = await asyncio.gather(*[
        c.completions.create(model='/models', prompt=p, max_tokens=120)
        for p in prompts
    ])
    dt = time.perf_counter() - t0
    toks = sum(r.usage.completion_tokens for r in rs)
    print(f'{name:8s}  {n} req  {toks} tok  {dt:.2f}s  {toks/dt:,.0f} tok/s')

asyncio.run(bench('vLLM',   'http://localhost:8000/v1'))
asyncio.run(bench('SGLang', 'http://localhost:30000/v1'))
```

With a tiny model and no shared prefixes, expect rough parity. With long shared system prompts (as in agent/RAG workloads), SGLang's gap opens up.

sections.title: Debugging the Full Stack (training → kernel → server → endpoint)
sections.content:
The whole point of building this stack yourself is that when something breaks you have visibility at every layer. This section walks the debugging tools layer by layer and shows what a useful trace looks like.

### Layer 1: Training loop — `breakpoint()` and a `--debug` flag

The simplest tool first. Drop a `breakpoint()` in `train.py` to enter PDB at that line:

```python
for step in range(args.total_steps):
    x, y = get_batch('train')
    if step == 1 and args.debug:
        breakpoint()         # inspect x, y, model state
    _, loss = model(x, y)
    ...
```

Then `python train.py --debug`. In PDB: `p x.shape`, `p y[0, :10]`, `p model.tok_emb.weight.norm()`, `p loss`. **`n`** to step over, **`s`** to step into, **`c`** to continue. This catches 80% of training bugs — wrong shapes, wrong dtypes, NaN inputs, mis-loaded checkpoints.

### Layer 2: PyTorch profiler — where is the time going?

For a real perf trace of the training step:

```python
from torch.profiler import profile, schedule, tensorboard_trace_handler, ProfilerActivity

schedule = schedule(wait=1, warmup=3, active=5, repeat=1)
handler  = tensorboard_trace_handler('./profiler_out')

with profile(
    activities=[ProfilerActivity.CPU, ProfilerActivity.CUDA],
    schedule=schedule,
    on_trace_ready=handler,
    record_shapes=True,
    with_stack=True,
) as prof:
    for step in range(10):
        x, y = get_batch('train')
        _, loss = model(x, y)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        prof.step()
```

This dumps a Chrome-trace-formatted JSON into `./profiler_out`. Open it in **chrome://tracing** or in **TensorBoard** (`tensorboard --logdir ./profiler_out`). You'll see a per-kernel timeline. The most useful column is **"Self CUDA time"** in the operator summary — if a single op is >30% of step time, that's your next Triton/torch.compile target.

A typical first profile of this model shows:

```
Name                                    Self CUDA  CPU time  Calls
aten::scaled_dot_product_attention      28.4%       1.21ms    16
aten::linear                            22.1%       0.94ms    72
aten::cross_entropy                     10.8%       0.46ms     1
rms_norm_fwd_kernel (custom)             4.2%       0.18ms    16
aten::add                                4.0%       0.17ms    32
...
```

If you see `rms_norm_eager` at the top instead, your Triton swap didn't take effect — the conditional dispatch in `RMSNorm.forward` is failing somewhere.

### Layer 3: NVIDIA Nsight Systems — one level deeper

`nsys` traces kernel launches, memory copies, and CUDA stream timing at sub-microsecond resolution. Install via the CUDA toolkit, then:

```bash
nsys profile -o train_trace --capture-range=cudaProfilerApi \
    python train.py --total_steps 20
```

Wrap the steps you care about with `torch.cuda.cudart().cudaProfilerStart()` / `Stop()` to focus the capture on a single iteration. Open `train_trace.nsys-rep` in the Nsight Systems GUI. You will see:

- The CUDA stream timeline at microsecond resolution.
- Memory copies (HtoD, DtoH) — if you see lots of these per step, your data loader is on the wrong device.
- Gaps between kernels — kernel-launch overhead. Where you'd want CUDA graphs.
- Per-SM utilization heatmaps (if you also collect with `--gpu-metrics-device=all`).

**On Mac**, the equivalent is **Instruments → Metal System Trace**. The Apple MPS course modules walk through it.

### Layer 4: Container-internal debugging — `py-spy dump`

The vLLM container is running. It's slow, or it's deadlocked. You need a stack trace from outside.

```bash
# Find the pid inside the container
docker exec vllm pip install py-spy
docker exec vllm py-spy dump --pid 1
```

Or a live sampling profile:

```bash
docker exec vllm py-spy top --pid 1     # like `top`, but for Python frames
docker exec vllm py-spy record -o /tmp/profile.svg --pid 1 --duration 30
docker cp vllm:/tmp/profile.svg .
```

`py-spy` reads the process memory directly — it doesn't pause the target — so it's safe to run against a production server. The output flamegraph tells you where the Python scheduler is spending its time. (For the CUDA kernel time you'd combine this with `nsys` attached to the container, but that requires elevated capabilities.)

### Layer 5: vLLM debug flags

When vLLM is misbehaving, two flags peel off optimization layers so you can isolate the bug:

```bash
docker run ... my-vllm \
    --model /models \
    --enforce-eager \           # disables CUDA Graphs — slower but easier to interpret stack traces
    --disable-custom-all-reduce \
    --max-num-seqs 1            # single-request mode; removes batching from the picture
```

And to see what's happening at the request scheduler level, the **metrics endpoint**:

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/metrics | grep vllm_
# vllm:num_requests_running 1
# vllm:num_requests_waiting 0
# vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc 0.0123
# vllm:time_to_first_token_seconds_count ...
# vllm:time_per_output_token_seconds_count ...
```

The `gpu_cache_usage_perc` is gold — it tells you whether you're memory-bound (>90%) or wasting capacity (<30%).

### Layer 6: SGLang debug logs

SGLang has a verbose debug mode:

```bash
docker run ... my-sglang \
    --model-path /models \
    --log-level debug \
    --disable-radix-cache         # turn off RadixAttention to isolate it as a variable
```

With `debug` you'll see per-request scheduling decisions:

```
[DEBUG] new request rid=abc123 input_len=24 sampling_params=...
[DEBUG] schedule decode: bs=4 cache_hit=0.62 ...
[DEBUG] forward time = 8.2ms tokens generated = 4
```

The `cache_hit=0.62` means 62% of input tokens were served from the radix-tree cache — that's the metric that justifies SGLang's existence.

### Layer 7: HTTP layer — `curl -v` and `tcpdump`

If the model is producing tokens but your client isn't seeing them, the bug is in the HTTP stack. `curl -v` shows the raw request/response. For streaming endpoints, `curl -N` (no buffering) lets you see SSE chunks arrive in real time:

```bash
curl -N http://localhost:8000/v1/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"model":"/models","prompt":"Once","max_tokens":40,"stream":true}'
```

Each `data: {...}` line is one streamed chunk. If they aren't arriving in real time, an intermediate reverse proxy is buffering them — usually Nginx with the wrong `proxy_buffering off` config.

### A debugging recipe (when stuck)

Follow this sequence — it isolates the buggy layer in minutes:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  A["Symptom: server returns junk tokens"] --> B{"Can the model.generate() in Python\nproduce sensible tokens from the HF dir?"}
  B -- no --> CKPT["Bug is in the HF conversion.\nDiff state-dict keys, check dtype."]
  B -- yes --> C{"Does vLLM startup show\n'Loading model weights took X GB'\nmatching what you expect?"}
  C -- no --> CONFIG["config.json mismatch.\nCheck hidden_size, n_heads, vocab_size."]
  C -- yes --> D{"Single-request curl with --max-num-seqs 1\n--enforce-eager works?"}
  D -- no --> KERN["Bug is in vLLM's kernel for your shape.\nFile an issue with the trace."]
  D -- yes --> E["Bug is in batching / KV cache / paged attn.\nIncrease memory-utilization,\ndisable prefix caching, retry."]
```

### Closing thought

The ability to debug an LLM serving stack end-to-end is a superpower nobody is born with. Every layer in this section is just a tool — `breakpoint`, `torch.profiler`, `nsys`, `py-spy`, `--log-level debug`, `curl -v` — and you put them together when the problem demands. The reason this module had you build the whole stack yourself is that you now know every layer well enough to know **which tool to reach for** when the bug shows up.

sections.title: What You Just Built — and What's Next
sections.content:
Step back. You built this:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  TS["TinyStories\n2.1M stories"] --> TOK["Llama 32k BPE\ntokenization"]
  TOK --> BIN["train.bin\nuint16 memmap"]
  BIN --> MOD["TinyLlama\n12M params\nRMSNorm+SwiGLU+RoPE"]
  MOD --> TRAIN["AdamW + cosine\nbf16 + torch.compile\n45k tok/s on a 4090"]
  TRAIN --> KERN["Custom Triton\nfused RMSNorm\n~2x faster"]
  KERN --> CKPT["checkpoint.pt"]
  CKPT --> HF["LlamaForCausalLM\nsafetensors\nHF format"]
  HF --> VLLM["vLLM Docker\nPagedAttention\n/v1/completions"]
  HF --> SGL["SGLang Docker\nRadixAttention\n@sgl.function"]
  VLLM --> APP["openai SDK\ncurl\nyour app"]
  SGL --> APP
```

Every box is a real thing you wrote or configured. Not pseudocode, not screenshots, not toy demos — actual artifacts you can re-run tomorrow. **That is what end-to-end means**.

### The layers, named

| Layer | Artifact | What you now understand |
|---|---|---|
| Data | `train.bin`, `val.bin` | Tokenizer-as-part-of-model, packed memmap loading, the nanoGPT pattern |
| Model | `model.py` (~150 LOC) | Llama internals: RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, weight tying, why no bias |
| Training | `train.py` | Device picker, AdamW + cosine warmup, MFU, torch.compile, checkpointing |
| Kernels | Triton RMSNorm or compiled MPS path | Custom-kernel workflow, benchmarking, swap-in dispatch |
| Checkpoint | `ckpt_final.pt` | What a state_dict actually contains |
| Conversion | `to_hf.py` | Why naming conventions matter, how production servers load weights |
| Serving (vLLM) | Docker container, `:8000` | PagedAttention, OpenAI API shim, GPU memory utilization tuning |
| Serving (SGLang) | Docker container, `:30000` | RadixAttention, constrained decoding, `@sgl.function` DSL |
| Debugging | `torch.profiler`, `nsys`, `py-spy`, vLLM metrics | Which tool to reach for at which layer |

### Scaling this up

This is the moment to step into bigger things. A short menu, ordered by ROI:

#### 1) QLoRA on Llama-3-8B on a single 4090

The single highest-leverage next step. You don't need to retrain a base model — you finetune a strong pretrained one on your own data. With QLoRA (4-bit base model + LoRA adapters in fp16), a Llama-3-8B fits in 14 GB and trains at meaningful throughput on a single 24 GB card. The HuggingFace `peft` library and `bitsandbytes` handle it in ~20 lines of config. Output: a few-MB LoRA adapter that captures your finetune, loadable in vLLM via `--enable-lora`.

#### 2) Quantization with GGUF / AWQ / FP8

Once you have a finetune you like, quantize it. **GGUF** (the llama.cpp format) is the path for edge / CPU / Mac deployments. **AWQ** and **GPTQ** give you INT4 weights for GPU inference at ~2x the throughput of bf16. **FP8** (H100/B200 only) is the production sweet spot: nearly-no quality cost and hardware-native acceleration. vLLM and SGLang both load all four formats with a flag.

#### 3) Multi-GPU tensor parallel

To serve a 70B model, you need 2-4 GPUs with **tensor parallelism**. Both vLLM (`--tensor-parallel-size 4`) and SGLang (`--tp 4`) handle it. Read the model file once, shard along the head dimension, all-reduce after each matmul. The math you'd write to do this yourself is in the PyTorch course's distributed module.

#### 4) RLHF / DPO finetuning

The path from "helpful base model" to "chatbot" runs through preference learning. The RL course (`rl03-rlhf-dpo`) goes through this in detail. Once you have a chat-style finetune you can apply DPO with a few thousand human preference pairs and get a noticeably better assistant.

#### 5) Watching the leaderboards

Where the field is going right now (as of mid-2026):

- **HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard** — the public scoreboard for open base models. Watch the small-model (3B, 7B) rows; that's where you can compete.
- **LMSYS Arena (Chatbot Arena)** — the human-preference leaderboard for chat models. The gap between open and closed is now ~10%.
- **MTEB** — embedding/retrieval benchmarks. Closer to your RAG workloads.
- **AlpacaEval / Arena-Hard** — automated instruction-following benchmarks. Faster iteration than human-rated.

### What you couldn't have known at the start

Few things land differently after going through this:

- **Inference engineering is a stack, not a trick.** PagedAttention multiplies with continuous batching multiplies with FP8 multiplies with speculative decoding. Skipping a layer leaves 5-10x on the table.
- **The HF format is the contract.** Your custom training code can do anything as long as the final `state_dict` matches the canonical Llama keys. Production inference will never run your training code.
- **The smallest models are the most pedagogical.** 12M params is small enough to debug in your head and big enough to need every real optimization. Scaling up doesn't introduce new concepts — it just stresses the same ones harder.
- **The tokenizer is part of the model.** Pin it at the data-prep step or pay later.
- **Docker is the deployment substrate, not the optimization.** All the perf is in the kernel and scheduler layers. Docker just makes it portable.

### The view from the other side

You now own the full stack. When a paper drops claiming a 2x speedup from a new attention variant, you know exactly where to plug it in. When a colleague's vLLM server returns nonsense after a finetune, you know to check the state-dict keys first. When someone asks "can we self-host this?" you can give a real answer with a real cost estimate.

The rest of the field is just bigger versions of what you just built. **Go build them.**

quiz.question: Why does this module pick the Llama 32k SentencePiece tokenizer over `tiktoken` (GPT-2 BPE) for data prep — even though tiktoken is faster and would also produce a trainable model?
options: tiktoken doesn't support uint16 encoding.
tiktoken vocabularies are too large to fit in 16-bit token IDs.

The tokenizer is part of the model. vLLM and SGLang load the model as `LlamaForCausalLM`, which expects Llama's SentencePiece tokenizer files. Mismatch between training and serving tokenizers silently scrambles every prompt the server receives — a tokenizer pinned at data-prep time avoids this entire class of bug.
tiktoken doesn't run on MPS.
quiz.explanation: Tokenizer = model. If you train on cl100k BPE and serve as `LlamaForCausalLM`, every server-side prompt is encoded by Llama's SentencePiece BPE — a different sequence of token IDs than the model trained on. The forward pass runs fine; the outputs are garbage. Pinning the Llama tokenizer at data-prep is the simplest fix.

quiz.question: Llama uses RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm. What's the practical difference, and why does it matter for a small transformer like our 12M-param model?
options: RMSNorm uses learned biases; LayerNorm doesn't.

RMSNorm drops the mean-centering step and the bias parameter, leaving just `x / sqrt(mean(x²) + ε) * gamma`. One fewer reduction per call, ~10-30% faster in practice, no measurable quality loss. For a small bandwidth-bound model, faster normalization is a real wall-clock win.
RMSNorm normalizes across the batch dimension; LayerNorm normalizes across the feature dimension.
RMSNorm uses a learned epsilon.
quiz.explanation: LayerNorm: `(x - mean) / sqrt(var + ε) * gamma + beta`. RMSNorm: `x / sqrt(mean(x²) + ε) * gamma`. No mean subtraction, no bias. One reduction instead of two. Zhang & Sennrich (2019) and the Llama papers found no quality cost. On bandwidth-bound small models this is one of the cleanest free-lunch substitutions in transformer architecture.

quiz.question: After training, you convert your PyTorch checkpoint to HuggingFace format with a custom remap script (`to_hf.py`). Why is this step necessary even though vLLM and SGLang are both Python?
options: PyTorch checkpoints aren't compatible with safetensors.
vLLM doesn't support custom architectures.

Production inference servers do NOT load your training code. They reimplement the architecture in their own optimized path (vLLM has its own paged-attention-aware Llama implementation). They then load your weights into THEIR module by parameter name. The safetensors file must use the canonical Llama key names (`model.layers.{i}.self_attn.q_proj.weight`, etc.) — your training code's naming (`blocks.{i}.attn.wq.weight`) won't load.
HuggingFace requires it for licensing.
quiz.explanation: This is the single most underestimated production gotcha. vLLM has its own `LlamaForCausalLM` tuned for PagedAttention; SGLang has its own tuned for RadixAttention. Both load weights by canonical Llama parameter names. Your training-side names don't ship. The remap in `to_hf.py` is what makes the model loadable. Get the key names wrong and the server loads fine but produces gibberish — silent failure.

quiz.question: vLLM's PagedAttention is best described as:
options: A faster attention math kernel that reduces FLOPs.

A block-level KV cache allocator inspired by OS paged virtual memory. It divides the KV cache into fixed-size blocks, maintains a per-request block table mapping logical token positions to physical blocks, allocates lazily, reclaims on completion, and enables prefix sharing across requests. The attention kernel itself is essentially unchanged.
A compression scheme for KV cache values.
A new attention pattern that replaces softmax.
quiz.explanation: The vLLM paper's title literally says "Efficient Memory Management." PagedAttention is a memory allocator. It eliminates fragmentation (20-40% of KV memory was wasted in pre-paged engines), enables lazy growth (requests don't pre-allocate `max_seq_len`), and enables prefix sharing. The custom attention kernel that reads non-contiguous blocks has ~2-4% overhead — vastly outweighed by the memory wins.

quiz.question: SGLang's RadixAttention beats vLLM's PagedAttention most strongly on which workload?
options: Single-tenant inference with one request at a time.

Workloads with high token-prefix sharing across requests — RAG with reused retrieved chunks, agent loops with shared system prompts, multi-turn chat. The radix tree automatically detects token-prefix overlap and reuses the cached KV subtree, giving 3-10x throughput over vLLM on prefix-heavy workloads with no application-side cache management.
Workloads with very long generations and short prompts.
Mobile / edge deployments.
quiz.explanation: Where vLLM's prefix cache hashes the first N blocks of each prompt looking for exact-match reuse, RadixAttention stores all KV in a token-keyed radix tree — so ANY two requests sharing a prefix reuse the cached subtree automatically.
